\begin{enumerate}

  \item Textbook Exercise 6.3.1c
  \begin{solution}

\begin{parts}
\setcounter{partno}{2}
\part Let $u_1 = x$, $u_2 = x'$, $u_3 = y$, $u_4 = y'$. Then:
\begin{align*}
u_1' &= u_2\\
u_2' &= x'' = -\frac{ax}{(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}} = -\frac{au_1}{(u_1^2+u_3^2)^{3/2}}\\
u_3' &= u_4\\
u_4' &= y'' = -\frac{ay}{(x^2+y^2)^{3/2}} = -\frac{au_3}{(u_1^2+u_3^2)^{3/2}}
\end{align*}

Initial conditions: $u_1(0) = 1$, $u_2(0) = 0$, $u_3(0) = 0$, $u_4(0) = 3$.

In vector form with $\mathbf{u} = [u_1, u_2, u_3, u_4]^T$:
$$\mathbf{u}' = \begin{bmatrix} u_2 \\ -\frac{au_1}{(u_1^2+u_3^2)^{3/2}} \\ u_4 \\ -\frac{au_3}{(u_1^2+u_3^2)^{3/2}} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{u}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

\end{parts}

\end{solution}
  \item Textbook Exercise 6.3.2c
\begin{solution}

\begin{parts}
\setcounter{partno}{2}
\part Let $u_1 = y$, $u_2 = y'$. From $2x^2y'' + 3xy' - y = 0$:
$$y'' = \frac{y - 3xy'}{2x^2} = \frac{u_1 - 3xu_2}{2x^2}$$

System with $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix} u_1 \\ u_2 \end{bmatrix}$:
$$\mathbf{u}' = \mathbf{f}(x, \mathbf{u}) = \begin{bmatrix} u_2 \\ \frac{u_1 - 3xu_2}{2x^2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{u}(2) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Euler's method: $\mathbf{u}^{(n+1)} = \mathbf{u}^{(n)} + h\mathbf{f}(x_n, \mathbf{u}^{(n)})$

\textbf{Step 1:} $x_0 = 2$, $\mathbf{u}^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix}$, $h = 1/8$

$$\mathbf{f}(2, \mathbf{u}^{(0)}) = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 \\ \frac{1 - 3(2)(-1/2)}{2(4)} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{u}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1/2 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{8}\begin{bmatrix} -1/2 \\ 1/2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 15/16 \\ -7/16 \end{bmatrix}$$

\textbf{Step 2:} $x_1 = 17/8$, $\mathbf{u}^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix} 15/16 \\ -7/16 \end{bmatrix}$

$$\mathbf{f}(17/8, \mathbf{u}^{(1)}) = \begin{bmatrix} -7/16 \\ \frac{15/16 - 3(17/8)(-7/16)}{2(17/8)^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -7/16 \\ 477/1156 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{u}^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 15/16 \\ -7/16 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{8}\begin{bmatrix} -7/16 \\ 477/1156 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 113/128 \\ -7/16 + 477/9248 \end{bmatrix}$$

At $x_2 = 9/4$: $\widehat{y}(9/4) = 8/9 \approx 0.8889$

Numerical approximation: $u_1^{(2)} = 113/128 \approx 0.8828$

$$\text{Error} = \left|\frac{8}{9} - \frac{113}{128}\right| = \frac{7}{1152} \approx 0.0061$$

\end{parts}

\end{solution}


\item Textbook Exercise 6.6.2a
  \begin{solution}

\begin{parts}
\part Backward Euler (AM1) formula: $u_1 = u_0 + hf(t_1, u_1)$

Given: $f(t,u) = -2tu$, $u_0 = 2$, $h = 0.2$, $t_0 = 0$, $t_1 = 0.2$

$$u_1 = u_0 + hf(t_1, u_1) = 2 + 0.2(-2(0.2)u_1)$$

$$u_1 = 2 - 0.08u_1$$

$$u_1 + 0.08u_1 = 2$$

$$1.08u_1 = 2$$

$$u_1 = \frac{2}{1.08} = \frac{200}{108} = \frac{50}{27} \approx 1.852$$

\end{parts}

\end{solution}
  \item Textbook Exercise 6.6.3a
\begin{solution}

\begin{parts}
\part Trapezoidal rule (AM2) formula: $u_1 = u_0 + \frac{h}{2}[f(t_0, u_0) + f(t_1, u_1)]$

Given: $f(t,u) = -2tu$, $u_0 = 2$, $h = 0.2$, $t_0 = 0$, $t_1 = 0.2$

First, compute $f(t_0, u_0) = f(0, 2) = -2(0)(2) = 0$

$$u_1 = 2 + \frac{0.2}{2}[0 + (-2(0.2)u_1)]$$

$$u_1 = 2 + 0.1(-0.4u_1)$$

$$u_1 = 2 - 0.04u_1$$

$$u_1 + 0.04u_1 = 2$$

$$1.04u_1 = 2$$

$$u_1 = \frac{2}{1.04} = \frac{200}{104} = \frac{50}{26} = \frac{25}{13} \approx 1.923$$

\end{parts}

\end{solution}

  \item Consider the IVP 
  \begin{align*}
    u' = -16u, \hspace{2em}
    u(0) = 1,
  \end{align*}
  with exact solution $\widehat{u}(t) = e^{-16t}$. We'll compare the local truncation errors of modified Euler which has the Butcher tableau 
  \begin{equation*}
  \begin{array}{c|cc}
  0 & 0 & 0 \\
  1 & 1 & 0 \\
  \hline
  & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{2}
  \end{array}
  \end{equation*}
  and trapezoidal rule.
  \begin{parts}
    \part Write down the formula for $u_1$ for the modified Euler method. Call this $u_1^{\text{ME}}$.
    \part Write down the formula for $u_1$ for the trapezoidal rule. Call this $u_1^{\text{TR}}$.
    \part Compute the local truncation error for the modified Euler method, i.e. compute $\widehat{u}(h) - u_1^{\text{ME}}$. Simplify all the way to a power series in $h$. Express your final answer as $ch^p + O(h^{p+1})$ for some constant $c$ and integer $p$.
    \part Compute the local truncation error for the trapezoidal rule, i.e. compute $\widehat{u}(h) - u_1^{\text{TR}}$. Simplify all the way to a power series in $h$. Express your final answer as $ch^p + O(h^{p+1})$ for some constant $c$ and integer $p$.
    \part Which method has a smaller local truncation error for this problem?
    \begin{solution}

\begin{parts}
\part For modified Euler with $f(u) = -16u$:
\begin{align*}
k_1 &= f(u_0) = -16(1) = -16\\
k_2 &= f(u_0 + hk_1) = -16(1 + h(-16)) = -16(1 - 16h)\\
u_1^{\text{ME}} &= u_0 + \frac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2) = 1 + \frac{h}{2}(-16 + (-16)(1-16h))\\
&= 1 + \frac{h}{2}(-32 + 256h) = 1 - 16h + 128h^2
\end{align*}

\part For trapezoidal rule, $u_1^{\text{TR}}$ satisfies:
$$u_1^{\text{TR}} = u_0 + \frac{h}{2}(f(u_0) + f(u_1^{\text{TR}})) = 1 + \frac{h}{2}(-16(1) + (-16)u_1^{\text{TR}})$$
$$u_1^{\text{TR}} = 1 - 8h - 8hu_1^{\text{TR}}$$
$$(1 + 8h)u_1^{\text{TR}} = 1 - 8h$$
$$u_1^{\text{TR}} = \frac{1-8h}{1+8h}$$

\part The exact solution at $t = h$ is:
$$\widehat{u}(h) = e^{-16h} = 1 - 16h + \frac{256h^2}{2} - \frac{4096h^3}{6} + O(h^4) = 1 - 16h + 128h^2 - \frac{2048h^3}{3} + O(h^4)$$

Local truncation error for modified Euler:
\begin{align*}
\widehat{u}(h) - u_1^{\text{ME}} &= (1 - 16h + 128h^2 - \frac{2048h^3}{3} + O(h^4)) - (1 - 16h + 128h^2)\\
&= -\frac{2048h^3}{3} + O(h^4)
\end{align*}

\part For trapezoidal rule, expand $u_1^{\text{TR}}$:
$$u_1^{\text{TR}} = (1-8h)(1+8h)^{-1} = (1-8h)(1 - 8h + 64h^2 - 512h^3 + O(h^4))$$
$$= 1 - 8h + 64h^2 - 512h^3 - 8h + 64h^2 - 512h^3 + O(h^4)$$
$$= 1 - 16h + 128h^2 - 1024h^3 + O(h^4)$$

Local truncation error:
\begin{align*}
\widehat{u}(h) - u_1^{\text{TR}} &= (1 - 16h + 128h^2 - \frac{2048h^3}{3} + O(h^4)) - (1 - 16h + 128h^2 - 1024h^3 + O(h^4))\\
&= -\frac{2048h^3}{3} + 1024h^3 + O(h^4) = \left(1024 - \frac{2048}{3}\right)h^3 + O(h^4)\\
&= \frac{1024h^3}{3} + O(h^4)
\end{align*}

\part The trapezoidal rule has smaller local truncation error since $\left|\frac{1024}{3}\right| < \left|\frac{2048}{3}\right|$.

\end{parts}

\end{solution}
  \end{parts}

  \item Jupyter Notebook

\end{enumerate}

\section*{Quiz Information}

The quiz will be held on \fbox{\bf \color{red} Monday, April 27, 2026} instead of the usual lecture.

Quiz questions will be modifications of the {\it homework problems and material discussed in class}.

\textbf{Topics covered:}

Chapters 6.2 and 6.6

\begin{itemize}
  \item System of ODEs
  \item Implicit methods (not general multi-step methods, just the implicit Euler method and trapezoidal rule)
\end{itemize}

You should memorize the backward Euler method and trapezoidal rule. I'll provide a Butcher tableau for any explicit methods that I ask about on the quiz.
